';ji“"j;; In reply to Seamus ua Casaide’s query, may I state that my friend fi
" Mr. John Glynn, Tawn Clerk, Tuam, knows all about this short-lived rif‘fg._

—- a

I periodical. He oorresponded with Mr. James Ronan—who was a
' wholesale tea merchant in Manchester—at the time, and has kindly:
a.llowad me to make the appended extracts from his letters. The type
I wed in the production of the ‘‘Journal,” was designed by Canon
Bourke, and when it ceased 1t was sent here and used for printing :

=l the Gaelic portion of the ‘‘ Tuam News,”’” until it was worn out. It 4
, was from it that Canon Bourke’s edition of O’Gallagher’s ‘¢ Sermons,’* L
o published by M. H. Gill, in 1877, was printed. The preface to that B

88 in writing and editing Irish Gaelic.” - i

148 THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. _

to bm.ld it up. If the Irish nation can only claim and practice Purlty

and perfection of speech in the God-given and only language of the ‘!n i
Irish people, then, and not till then, will Irishmen be equal to tp, &
people of any other nation! Father Bourke, of St. Jarlath’s, is thy ?;; i :
only man who has as yet practically stepped into the breach. Tg 1 s
years or more have rolled over since he gave us his ‘ Kasy Lessong? ' :

No one came forward during that time to ‘put a shoulder to the wheq]’ {‘“‘ 5

i to assist him. I saw this, and it grieves me. Could I do anything tq
second his ‘ forlorn hope’? I was a man of commerce, not of letters. ; {
il ~ Still, after such a length of time his great effort seems to have sunk, §
f ks | and I calculated that if I did not step forward, nobody else would.
i* - And, after all, there was some force I could bring into the field. I had 2
; studied the ‘ Easy Lessons,” and had the advantage of the acquaintance . ﬁ: 1
2 with the system of self-instruction in other European languages. ,;
: Could something be brought out in Keltic which would combine all the | f :
E’ best points of Ollendorf, of Cassell, of Hall and of Ahn, and along with §

being the most simple and minute of all, to be at the same time a

grand piece of literary work? I wrote to Father Bourke—he gives
e himself heart and soul to the work. The ‘XKeltic Educator’ is our |
-.; attempt to realize the idea. The Keltic orthography is as yet very
unsettled. An attempt is being made to settle and fix it. Many de-
lays arise from this, the proof-sheets having to cross the channel
several times. No. 6 will be struck off to-morrow, as I expect the final -
preof from Tuam to-day. . . -. All the money received as yeb from
subscriptiors, stationers, etc., has been handed to the printer to m(aet
his bills. His last bill amounted to £15 more than had been received.
This I paid out of my own pocket, which with paying for adveltlﬁﬁ'
ments will be about £25, that I am so far lying out of. I musb not } i ﬁ
however go too far in this way. I must change (if necessary) my plan, 'w
but the work must be done! If through want of support we have to W
delay the publication, the setting up of the type and the stereotvypm&
will nevertheless go on quietly, to the end of the 52 mumbers. We 11
shall have to sink far less money in the plates than is required for i |
great quantities of paper and printing, for which the money has, 50“ :
far, not come back to us. When the work is omce in plate it will ﬂlen'
be easy to strike off all reqm'red n.umnbe.rs, and at a wwll-measm _,
cost. If the complete work be once placed mthm reach of the _?
| of Ireland, so that all may be enabled to learn to read and wn-_
' *i,aha].l have had my reward—all I lmk i‘t}r and no sac.r fice son)
too gmb for 'bhle, end.”

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. 149

But the high hopes of this enthusjast were doomed to disappoint-
! ment. Wnting ‘tao Mr. Glynn two years later, 17th August, 1871, he
- sorrowfully admits that ‘““only seven numbers of the journal have as

vet been printed,”” and that h¢ has a large quantity on hand.. Nome the

yf less should his efforts be chronicled, and hig name preserved, as one
vho, seeing the old tongue dying out, did all that in him lay to avert
that disaster.

Tuam. T. B. COSTELLO, M.D.

PRINTING IN TRALEE, 1801-30.

Subjoined is a list of such items of printing in Tralee durine this
period as I have beem able to note down. It will be observed that, so
. far as I can discover, there was no book and only one pamphlet_ printed

L there in all those years. This hardly secems credible, and perhaps the

appearance of this meagre list will stimulate some patriotic Kerryman
0 make further research, and, if fortunate emou gh to make any dis-
coveries, to send the result to you. E. R. McC. DIX.
1505, March 26th. Chute’s Western Herald. Vol. XII., No. 95, 4 cols.
each. Tuesday. Price 5d. (T.C.D., in vol. of odd news-
papers.)

AN “IRISH” TIMES.

The world is moving on—there’s no denying it. Who, ten, or even
five years ago would havé been bold enough to predict, or could possibly

Thunderer,’”’ g0 named by one Irishman, should, under the control of
another, publish a supplement of 32 pages boldly dubbed * The Irish
Nulmber,’ > golely devoted to the consideration of things Irish?  Yet
 6uch is the case. Wonders will never cease. It makes old fogeys rub
~ their eyes and exclaim, ‘Do I sleep, do I dream, or are visions

about ”’ ?. Every subject under the sun—barring politics—is dealt with,

" and every article is marked by a rare sympathy and insight, eaca De-
mg, we are assured and can well believe, ‘‘the work of writers fully

co to mention a few of those more

lish Imitations 7 ; * Glendakmgh ;s “The Royal Dublin Society Its'

Varied Activities and Public Us e:ﬁuln ess,”’ concluding with ‘“A By,

liography of Recent Book about Ireland.”  Several maps and many
illustrations are included in this wonderful production. When one ro

members the former attitude of ‘‘ The Times’ towards O’Conne]),
Parnell and indeed every other popular Irish leader, during a whole
contury ome may be pardored for thinking that this St. Patrick’s Day
issue is a sbep forward than can never be retraced, marking a change

in the relationship of the twio countries, the herald of a new dawn

that will usher in bri ghtél' days for both.

THE WEE CQULD WIUAN.

‘““ Where are ye going, and you so wea?’”’
“Up to the glen the fairies to see.”
‘““ Down to the glen, I’m sure ye mane.”
““ Well! It’s up ’fore it’s down,’’ said the wee ould wean.

“U p in the hills there’s a white thorn tree,
An’ creatures as lovely as lovely can be,
- All dancin’ and music. Oh! there it is plain,

I’ll be late. Slan leat!” says the wee ould wean. d

Once, when the stars were shining bright,

And set the clouds racing mnto the night;

A sweet voice I knew rang out im the glen,

“T’ll be good. Oh! forgive.”” ’Twas the wee ould wean.

And 1nto my heart the reason why

Came to me as clear as the moon in the sky; |
She had told-what she saw, and begged for again.
She ma@ smﬂ‘*erian-g for tiha:t—-th-e' ,1)00&‘ wee wean.

And ‘T hoped for her sa ke, tmey would forgwe,
And show her again in what joy they live;
For her life was hard and -d'r-,r in the g:ra«l:n

- Mo lubfm ba:n my po-crr wee wean.

THE IRISH BOOK LOVER. . 153

IRISH LITERARY SOCIETY

The principal attraction of the Original Night on Saturday, 22nd
February, was a clever little Irish play, ‘‘ A Quare Way of Coortin’,”
the authoress—Miss Anna MacClure Warnock—and thé cast being :i.ll
members of the S_-oc.iety. The sceue 1s laid in Donegal, and the heroine,
Margaret Lee, belonging to the farming class, is so conceited by rea~
son of her ‘‘book-learning’ that she is rather contemptuous in her
sttitude towards Joe M’Keague, who is wooing her. The young farmer,
who is no scholar, by adopting the ruse of asking Miss Lee to write ‘ab
his dictation a letter conveying a proposal of marriage to a rival lady,
brings the heroine to a more reasonable frame of mind. The idea is
skilfully and humorously worked out; the fcur persons in the play are
admirably drawn types of Irish character, and the Donegal accent is
very much. in evidence. Miss Janet Perry as Margaret Lee, Mr. T. D.

- Fitzgerald as Joe M’Keague, Mr. E. W. Lynam as a village philosopher,
and Mrs. Martin Freeman as Mrs. M’Keague, acquitted themselves
excellently. The staging, though simple, was very effective. TI.M.

It was most appropriate that a poetess in the person of Miss Emily
Hickey chould preside at the Tea Table on Wednesday, 26th February,
whilst another poet, Mr. T. W. Rollesten, talked admirably and enter-
tainingly on the life and works of a third—Sir Aubrey de Vere. Little
is now known of the poems of this author, who was born at Curragh
Chase, was a schoolfellow with Byron at Harrow and a life-long friend
of Wordsworth. Indeed, he is cften confounded with, and his poems
attributed to, his more gifted son, Aubrey. Yet, judging by the ex-
tracts read by Mr. Rolleston from his plays and sonnets, they deserve
a better fate. During his lifetime they gai ned the approbation of the
seloct few, hut his historical dramas were unsuited for the stage owing
to their great l:en gth and want of techmque. They were mnever re-
printed, and are now most difficult to obtain, even the British Museum

1"'fﬁklng one, They are: «“ Julian, the Apostate,” a drama, 1822 ; the

“Duke ‘of Mercia,”’ an historical drama; *‘ The Lamentations of Ire-
land,”’ 1823 ; “ The Song of Faith,” 1842 ; ‘“ Mary Tudor;”’ an historical
drama, which, though written in 1844, was not published till 1847, a year
aftor the author’s death, and a volume of ““ Sonnets,” collected by his
son in 1875. An interesting discussion followed, taken pant in by Miss.

Sir Horace Plunkett delivered an address on Saturday, 15th March,
‘on ‘‘ The Irish. Farmer in a New Role.”” Mr. Harold Cox, editor of
‘the ‘‘ Edinburgh Review,’”’ presided. Sir Horace said that it had come
to be recognized that ever since the industrial revolution in England
-all the best thought of the country had been too much concentratel
upon problems of the urban population, to the neglect of the rura
populatron. Ior solution of the rural problem agriculture must be
regarded as an industry into which science must be brought, as into
-every other industry, and into the farmer’s business must be brought "i."
‘the same commercial methods as had beem found necessary to succes |
m every other busmess. They must also brighten the social and intel- |
dectual life of the people in the rural districts. The acceptance of thess |
propositions had led to the organization in America of a European I
Agricultural Commission for the purpose of vistting various Contin-
wntal countries, and giving particular attention to what was being dome
in Jreland. The Americans recognized that Ireland had given more
study and thought to this important economic pro-blem* than had any ' ,;
-other part of the United Kingdom. It was now known that the co-
woperative system, and not the joint stock system , was the only ome H
that appealed to the agricultural industry ; and it had also been proved
‘that the successful working of the co-operative methods of oombmamonf &
required cerbain associative qualities whichi the English had to leamn it
from the Irish. Tt was owmg to this fact that they had succeeded m
mtroducing the oo—Opera,twe system into Ireland. During twen’c-y—ﬁv@
years of work he had never felt that Ireland was in such a proud pOSI
. ‘tiom as it was to-day, and he rejoiced that the time had COme ‘whem’*

IrisH PLAYS AND PrAywricHTS. By Cornelius Weygandt.” (Con-

stable.) The author of this handsomely got-up book—an American

 scholar—writes most interestingly and enthusiastically of the modern

dramatic revival associated with the name of the A%bey Theatre. To

a close study of the plays themselves, he has added many details de-

| rived from a personal acquaintance with the authors and actors, many

1 of whom have lent him ready assistamce in compiling -his exhaustive

- work. Separate chapters are maturally devoted to the leaders, Mr.

| Yeats, Lady Gregory, and J. M. Synge, and one to the younger drama-

tists, to  whom eveny justice is done and all encouragement offered. It

| is enriched with many photographs, and the fullest details of the vari-

ous productions are chronicled with a minuteness that shows how much

the writer loves his subject; whilst an appendix gives the date, title

~ and author of every play produced from May, 1899, till November, 1912.

We venture to predict that this book will live, and that 1n years

. to como it will frequently be corsulted as a reliable work cf reference
"_f: . on the genesis, trials and triumphs of the Irish National Theatre,

It had never been my privilege to look upon Isaac Butt in the flesh,.
but I received from him, during a residence in Ireland, a single mark
of his courtesy, kindliness and promptness of action. I had solicited
his assistance to secure the admission of a student to certain classes in .
Dublin, and he readily responded to my request. ‘‘I will do anything
I can to help him,’’ he replied in a letter which lies before me, under
date “ December 8th, 1878’ (the year before his death). The letter
bears hig (I presume) family crest (whioch I should umn¥eraldically des-

cribe as a horse’s head crowned by what are known as Prinoe of Wales’

Patrick O’LEeARY, who, like Rev. E. MacCoy, was a good Gaelic
scholar, and the author of one work printed entirely from Irish type.
also deserves a miche in I.B.L. He was born near Castletown Bere. B
haven, Co. Cork, spole Trish from childhood, and by dint of hard study "f?;;
mastered the Old and Middle Irish. He became a schoolmaster near §
Dublin, and was often employed as a scribe and translator by another é
Co. Cork scholar—Rev. Edmund Hogan, S.J. O‘Leany’s collection of -
Munster folk tales, ‘‘Sgeuluident Cuige Mumain,” a thin octavo of |
124 pages, was printed by Patrick O’Brien, of Cuffe Street, Dublin, in |
1895. Shortly afterwards the poor fellow fell a victim to consumption,
and returned to his native district, where he passed away on 30th
October, 1896, at the early age of 26 years.

Queenstown. | | | J. Cn.

OBITUARY.

Lorp WOLSELEY. A:It;}mﬁg-h the late Lord Wolseley was essentially a

man of action, he deserves notice in these pages on account of his con- i
‘tributions to literature. Born at Golden Bridge Hiouse, Co. Dublin, on
4th Jumne, 1833, after a long series of military successes in both hemi-
spheres, and attaining to the highest honours in his profession, he
:(lied at Mentone on 26th March in his 80th year. His works are:—
The Soldier’s Pocket Book, first printed 1869, 16mo., 5th ed., 1886;
Field Pocket Book for the Auxiliary Forces, 16mo., 1873; Life of the

- Duke of Marlborough, 8vo., 1894 ; Decline and Fall of Napoleon, 2 vols.,
8vo., 1895 ; The Story of a Soldier’s Life, 2 vols, Svo., 1903. There is
a Memoir of Sir Garnet Wolseley, by Chas. R. Lowe, in 2 vols., SV'O“-;-,_;; "';:

FORTHCOMING WORKS.

The Council of the Henry Bradshaw Society, which was formed to
commemorate the great Irish bibliographer, have in view an edition
by Prof. H. J. Lawlor, T.C.D., of the Martyrology of Ricemarch, 3
manuscript whioh attracted the special attention of Henry Bradshaw.

One of the scarcest pamphiets dealing with Orangeism is *‘ A Short
History of the Battle of the Diamond . . . . from pensonal exper-
once and Authentic Documents.’”” (8vo., pp. iv. + 47.) It was written
by Sir Wm. Verner, M.P. for Co. Armagh from 1832 till 1873, and
privately printed in London in 1863. Mr. Greer, of the Ulster Gazette,
Armagh, will shontly issue a reprint of the original.

Mr. George Birmingham has a new book in preparation which will
deal with Irish humourists. It will be produced by Mr. Ioulis, of
Fdinburgh, who published the very successful ‘‘ Lighter Side of Irish
Life.”” He has also Leen entrusted with the Cahermoyle papers with
a view to writing the too long deferred life of Willlam Smith O’Brien.

Mr. Alfred Perceval Graves will publish through: Elkin, Mathews,
in the autumn, ‘‘Studies in Irish Liverature.”” It will consist of the
lectures on ‘‘ Ferguson,” * Petrie,”” * Mangan,” etc., delivered by
him in London, Dublin and Belfast, and one, a fresh study of the life
and work of Wm. Allingham.

Mr. Graves has another new work almost ready, to be entitled,
‘“ Harp Strings of tho Gael,”” consisting of his own translations,
pathetic, humourous, amatory, and descriptive, some of whioch have al-
ready appeared in the ‘‘Dublin Review,” ‘The Contemporany,’”’ and
our own pages, and some others by earlier soholars. It will be pub-
lished in a handsome volume by the Devin .Adair Company, Fifth
Avenue, Now York, at a cost of three dollars and a half. . |

Messrs. Harrap contemplate a now series of histories of ¢ Greal
Nations,” with illustrations in colour, half-tone and line. The volume
on. Germany has been entrusted to Mr. T. W. Rolleston, who spont
several years in that country and is well read in its literatﬁm, whilst
that on Treland will be the joint production of Miss Eleanor Hull and
Prof. Stanley Lane Poole, hoth able scholars and gifted writers.
